lessons.title: The Standard Model and Fundamental Particles

content.body: Particle physics studies the fundamental building blocks of matter and the forces between them. The Standard Model describes all known particles and fundamental forces.

Fundamental forces:
• Strong nuclear: binds quarks and nucleons (very short range)
• Electromagnetic: between charged particles (infinite range)
• Weak nuclear: radioactive decay (very short range)
• Gravitational: between masses (infinite range, weakest)

Fundamental particles:
Quarks: up, down, charm, strange, top, bottom
Leptons: electron, muon, tau and their neutrinos
Bosons (force carriers): photon, W±, Z⁰, gluon, Higgs

content.title: Quarks and Hadrons
content.body:
Proton: uud quarks (charge +2/3+2/3-1/3 = +1)
Neutron: udd quarks (charge +2/3-1/3-1/3 = 0)

Hadrons: particles made of quarks
Baryons: 3 quarks (proton, neutron)
Mesons: quark + antiquark (pions)

Antimatter: every particle has antiparticle with opposite charge
When matter meets antimatter → annihilation → gamma rays

content.title: Particle Accelerators
content.body:
Particle accelerators (LHC at CERN) accelerate particles to near-light speed and collide them.
Collisions create new particles from energy (E=mc²).
Detectors measure particle tracks, energies, and identities.
Discovered: Higgs boson (2012), quark structure, W and Z bosons

questions.question: The strong nuclear force
options: binds quarks together and holds nucleus together
questions.answer: binds quarks together and holds nucleus together
questions.explanation: Strong force: most powerful of four forces, acts over very short range (~10⁻¹⁵m), holds quarks in protons/neutrons and nucleons in nucleus.

questions.question: Which is NOT a fundamental force?
questions.explanation: Elastic force is not fundamental — it arises from electromagnetic forces between atoms. The four fundamental forces are gravity, EM, strong, weak.

questions.question: Quarks combine in threes to form
questions.explanation: Baryons are hadrons made of 3 quarks. Proton (uud) and neutron (udd) are baryons.

questions.question: A proton consists of quarks
questions.explanation: Proton = 2 up quarks + 1 down quark. Charge: 2(+2/3) + 1(-1/3) = +1.

questions.question: The Higgs boson is associated with
options: the field that gives particles mass
questions.answer: the field that gives particles mass
questions.explanation: The Higgs field permeates space; particles interacting with it gain mass. The Higgs boson is the quantum of this field — discovered at LHC in 2012.

questions.question: When matter meets antimatter
options: they annihilate, producing gamma ray photons
questions.answer: they annihilate, producing gamma ray photons
questions.explanation: Matter-antimatter annihilation: mass converted to energy (E=mc²) in the form of high-energy gamma photons.

questions.question: A meson is made of
questions.explanation: Mesons are hadrons containing one quark and one antiquark. They are unstable. Examples: pions, kaons.

questions.question: The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) accelerates particles to
questions.explanation: LHC accelerates protons to 99.9999991% of the speed of light. It cannot reach exactly c (relativistic constraint).

questions.question: The weak nuclear force is responsible for
questions.explanation: The weak force mediates beta decay: n → p + e⁻ + antineutrino. The W⁻ boson carries the weak interaction.

questions.question: Leptons include
options: electrons, muons, taus and their neutrinos
questions.answer: electrons, muons, taus and their neutrinos
questions.explanation: Leptons are fundamental fermions that don't experience the strong force. Six leptons: e⁻, μ⁻, τ⁻ and their three neutrinos.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Binding energy:
E_b = Δm × c²  (Δm = mass defect)
Mass defect = mass of separate nucleons - mass of nucleus
Binding energy per nucleon peaks at iron (Fe-56)

Fission: heavy nucleus splits
²³⁵U + n → fission products + 2-3 neutrons + energy
Chain reaction: each fission releases neutrons → more fissions
Critical mass: minimum needed for sustained chain reaction

content.title: Nuclear Power Station
content.body:
Reactor core: uranium fuel rods undergo controlled fission
Moderator: slows neutrons (graphite or water)
Control rods: absorb neutrons (cadmium or boron) → control rate
Coolant: removes heat (water, CO₂)
Heat → steam → turbine → generator → electricity
Containment building: concrete shield → safety

content.title: Nuclear Fusion
content.body:
H + H → He + energy (in stars)
Deuterium + Tritium → He + neutron + 17.6 MeV
Conditions: temperature >10⁸ K (plasma)
Methods: tokamak (magnetic confinement), inertial confinement
Advantages over fission: more energy per kg, less radioactive waste, fuel abundant
Challenge: containing plasma hot enough

questions.question: Mass defect in a nucleus is
options: difference between mass of separate nucleons and nucleus mass
questions.answer: difference between mass of separate nucleons and nucleus mass
questions.explanation: Mass defect: mass of individual protons+neutrons minus nuclear mass. This missing mass becomes binding energy via E=mc².

questions.question: Binding energy per nucleon peaks at
questions.explanation: Iron-56 has the highest binding energy per nucleon — most stable nucleus. Elements lighter or heavier release energy moving toward Fe.

questions.question: Nuclear fission releases energy because
options: daughter nuclei have higher binding energy per nucleon than parent
questions.answer: daughter nuclei have higher binding energy per nucleon than parent
questions.explanation: Fission splits heavy nucleus (below Fe stability peak) → products with higher BE/nucleon → energy released.

questions.question: A chain reaction in fission is sustained when
options: each fission event produces enough neutrons to cause at least one more fission
questions.answer: each fission event produces enough neutrons to cause at least one more fission
questions.explanation: Chain reaction: 1 fission → 2-3 neutrons → each causes another fission → exponential increase (unless controlled).

questions.question: Control rods in a nuclear reactor
options: absorb neutrons to regulate the fission rate
questions.answer: absorb neutrons to regulate the fission rate
questions.explanation: Cadmium or boron control rods absorb neutrons. Inserting rods → fewer neutrons → slower reaction. Full insertion stops it.

questions.question: The moderator in a reactor slows neutrons to
options: thermal energies suitable for triggering fission in U-235
questions.answer: thermal energies suitable for triggering fission in U-235
questions.explanation: Fast neutrons are less effective at triggering U-235 fission. Moderator (water, graphite) slows them to 'thermal' energies.

questions.question: Fusion requires extremely high temperature because
options: protons must overcome electrostatic repulsion — high KE needed to get close enough
questions.answer: protons must overcome electrostatic repulsion — high KE needed to get close enough
questions.explanation: Positive nuclei repel each other. At >10⁸ K, kinetic energy is high enough to overcome Coulomb barrier and allow nuclear fusion.

questions.question: Fusion produces more energy than fission per kg of fuel because
options: light nuclei have low binding energy per nucleon — large gain moving toward Fe peak
questions.answer: light nuclei have low binding energy per nucleon — large gain moving toward Fe peak
questions.explanation: Fusion of light nuclei (H→He) releases ~4× more energy per kg than fission of heavy nuclei.

questions.question: A tokamak is a device for
options: confining hot plasma magnetically for fusion experiments
questions.answer: confining hot plasma magnetically for fusion experiments
questions.explanation: Tokamak: doughnut-shaped (toroidal) magnetic confinement vessel that holds 100+ million degree fusion plasma.

questions.question: Radioactive waste from fission is mainly
options: long-lived isotopes requiring storage for thousands of years
questions.answer: long-lived isotopes requiring storage for thousands of years
questions.explanation: Fission products include long-lived radioactive isotopes (e.g. Cs-137, Sr-90) that require secure storage for 10,000+ years.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Particle Physics. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Particle Physics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Particle Physics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 3.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Particle Physics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 3.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Particle Physics. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.
Common examination questions on Particle Physics include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Particle Physics. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
